current_fact.fact_text: On March 26, 2005, between 200,000 and 300,000 people filled the streets of Taipei to protest China’s Anti‑Secession Law, which authorized "non‑peaceful" means to stop Taiwanese independence. The march was a sea of color and homemade signs, a reminder that abstract legal texts can send real people outdoors to stand together.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

current_fact.match_reason: A mass protest against an anti‑secession law pairs naturally with an episode about Rage Against the Machine, whose music has long been a soundtrack to streets filled with dissent.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 2010, the South Korean corvette ROKS Cheonan broke in two and sank near the maritime border with North Korea, killing 46 sailors. An international investigation concluded it was hit by a North Korean torpedo, turning a dark, icy night at sea into a geopolitical flashpoint.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A warship lost in cold northern waters and an episode about Arctic diving with whales both inhabit the same frigid seascapes, one tragic, one awe‑filled, reminding us how the ocean can hold both conflict and wonder.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 2017, anti‑corruption protests swept across 99 Russian cities, many led by young people holding rubber ducks and blue sneakers as quiet symbols of elite excess. Polling later suggested that over a third of Russians sympathized with the demonstrations, and most held Vladimir Putin personally responsible for high‑level corruption.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: Citizens stepping into the streets to challenge entrenched power parallel an episode about breaking boxes and narratives; both lean on sharp, conscious voices to question the status quo.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1021, the Fatimid court finally announced that the caliph al‑Hakim bi‑Amr Allah had died — six weeks after he mysteriously disappeared during a nighttime ride near Cairo. The delay, and the strange end of a ruler already famed for eccentric decrees, helped fuel centuries of rumor, legend, and even new religious movements.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: A secretive caliphal death in medieval Cairo and an episode that begins in a medina with Arabic melodies share the same atmospheric alleyways of North African and Middle Eastern mystery.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1942, the first female prisoners arrived at the Auschwitz concentration camp in Nazi‑occupied Poland. Many were Polish political prisoners and resistance members, and their arrival marked the expansion of Auschwitz from a prison into a vast machinery of gendered terror and extermination.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: The unspeakable suffering of women imprisoned at Auschwitz is honored with an episode that uplifts powerful female voices; the pairing aims to remember those silenced by letting other women sing loudly in their place.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 2017, the Levada Center reported that 38% of surveyed Russians supported the anti‑corruption protests that had just swept the country, and 67% believed Vladimir Putin bore personal responsibility for high‑level corruption. In a system built on centralized power, the data itself became a quiet act of resistance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: An independent pollster measuring public anger at corruption fits with an episode about Residente, whose lyrics are a running audit of power and anthems for those who feel counted out.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1979, as politicians signed the Egypt–Israel peace treaty in Washington, D.C., millions watched on television worldwide, turning a diplomatic handshake into a shared planetary moment. For many in the Middle East, seeing flags of countries that had fought multiple wars flutter side by side was as surreal as any fiction.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

queue.match_reason: A televised peace ceremony in a global capital and an episode about walking through London both frame big political and personal moments against the backdrop of world cities where histories cross.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1958, Explorer 3's instruments once again traced the invisible Van Allen belts, showing that Earth is wrapped in dynamic rings of charged particles trapped by its magnetic field. These belts pose a hazard to spacecraft and astronauts, yet they also act as planetary armor, deflecting cosmic radiation that would make life far harsher on the surface.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Invisible radiation belts circling the planet feel like a cosmic version of trip‑hop's hazy, enveloping atmospheres — unseen layers that nonetheless shape everything moving through them.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1979, as peace was inked in Washington and planes crossed continents, countless small, unrecorded moments unfolded in kitchens, buses, and bedrooms around the world. Every timestamped treaty and headline shares its date with invisible personal milestones — a quiet reminder that history is both the grand and the deeply ordinary.

episode.title: Morning Drops #15 - Unfreezing
episode.description: A short, honest Morning Drop with no grand theme — just four tracks, a bit of voice, and the decision to not wait for the 'right' moment. Room to breathe today.
songs: Jane Birkin & Serge Gainsbourg - Je t’aime… moi non plus
Bernard Badie - In Love (DJ Quad's at Night Mix)

queue.match_reason: A reflection on all the unrecorded events that share a date with famous ones matches an episode about dropping perfectionism and 'grand themes' — honoring the small, real moments that rarely make history books.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1958, the United States launched Explorer 3, a small satellite that quietly carried a cosmic secret: it confirmed the existence of the Van Allen radiation belts around Earth. Built in just a few months, the mission turned what was once invisible space hazard into a mapped, measurable phenomenon.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A satellite mapping invisible space around Earth pairs with an episode about reimagining emptiness as creative space – both invite us to see 'empty' voids as full of possibility.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1700, English navigator William Dampier completed the first European circumnavigation of New Britain, proving it was an island separate from New Guinea. Pirate, explorer, and accidental naturalist, he filled his journals with some of the earliest European descriptions of Pacific flora, fauna, and peoples.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: An 18th‑century circumnavigation echoes in an episode recorded from a boat, both built around long days at sea, shifting horizons, and stories carried on waves.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1640, the Royal Academy of Turku was founded in what is now Finland, becoming the country's first university. For nearly two centuries it acted as a northern lighthouse of scholarship, surviving wars and fires before its legacy continued as the University of Helsinki.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: The founding of Finland's first university fits naturally with a reflective episode on the joy and difficulty of learning, both honoring knowledge as a long, evolving journey.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1812, a devastating magnitude 7.7 earthquake struck Caracas, Venezuela, collapsing churches mid‑mass and triggering landslides that reshaped the surrounding valleys. The disaster became entangled with politics and faith, as some leaders framed it as divine judgment while others saw it as a harsh lesson in geology, not theology.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A catastrophic earthquake and an episode about weathering storms both deal with ruptures in the ground beneath us and the fragile, hopeful work of rebuilding afterward.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1885, the Métis of the District of Saskatchewan, led by Louis Riel, began the North-West Rebellion against the Canadian government. It was a short, intense uprising fueled by land rights, broken promises, and the refusal of a mixed Indigenous people to let their culture be quietly erased.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A struggle by the Métis to defend land and identity resonates with Tuareg music born from exile and resistance, both telling stories of borderlands and survival in their own rhythms.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1939, Spanish Nationalist forces launched their final offensive of the Spanish Civil War, a swift campaign that shattered the last Republican resistance. Within days Madrid fell, and with it, Spain slipped into a decades‑long dictatorship under Francisco Franco.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: The brutal close of the Spanish Civil War finds an echo in an episode about flamenco, a music deeply shaped by Spain's history of pain, repression, and fierce emotional expression.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1954, the United States detonated the "Romeo" thermonuclear test at Bikini Atoll as part of Operation Castle, yielding about 11 megatons of explosive power. The blast was far larger than predicted, a stark reminder that in the early nuclear age, even the scientists were still guessing at the scale of their own creations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A thermonuclear blast and an 'Electric Shock' set both revolve around raw, overwhelming energy — one terrifying, one musical, each crackling with power beyond easy control.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1955, Pan Am Flight 845/26, a Boeing Stratocruiser crossing the Pacific, lost an engine and was forced to ditch into rough seas off the Oregon coast. In a feat of mid‑century aviation and human calm, the crew successfully landed on the ocean, and most passengers survived hours in life rafts before rescue.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: An airliner gently landing on the ocean and an episode about immersing below the surface both hover between fear and calm, turning open water into a place of suspended, listening time.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1970, South Vietnamese president Nguyễn Văn Thiệu launched a land reform program intended to shift farmland from large landlords to the peasants who actually worked it. In a war defined by ideology and foreign armies, this policy tried to quietly rewrite the ownership of the soil under everyone's feet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A policy aimed at slowly rebalancing who owns the land fits with an episode about slowing down and re‑grounding; both move away from chaos toward a more rooted, deliberate rhythm.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1971, East Pakistan declared independence from Pakistan, igniting the Bangladesh War of Independence. The proclamation, secretly recorded and broadcast over radio, turned a fragment of audio into the opening line of a new nation’s story.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: A revolutionary radio proclamation creating a new country aligns with an episode dissecting a misunderstood genre; both demystify how voices and rhythms can reshape political and cultural maps.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1979, Anwar Sadat of Egypt, Menachem Begin of Israel, and U.S. president Jimmy Carter signed the Egypt–Israel peace treaty in Washington, D.C. After decades of war, the document turned two bitter enemies into uneasy neighbors, and earned its architects both Nobel Prizes and assassination threats.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A landmark Middle Eastern peace treaty and an episode about travel‑induced perspective shifts both suggest that crossing borders — physical or mental — can turn conflict into cautious coexistence.

published.fact_text: Also on March 26, 1979, an Interflug Ilyushin Il‑18 attempted takeoff from Luanda's Quatro de Fevereiro Airport and crashed after a rejected liftoff, killing ten. The turboprop had been a workhorse of Eastern Bloc skies, and this accident highlighted how even routine departures can turn, mid‑runway, into emergencies.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: A takeoff that never quite happens contrasts poignantly with an 'On The Go' travel soundtrack; both sit in that liminal space between standing still and moving forward.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1991, Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay, and Paraguay signed the Treaty of Asunción, creating Mercosur, a common market intended to let goods and, to a degree, people move more freely across South America's southern cone. It turned national borders into softer lines for trade, knitting economies together with tariffs and treaties instead of railways.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A regional economic bloc blending four nations' interests mirrors a musical genre stitching together jazz, funk, and African rhythms; both are experiments in integration across borders.

published.fact_text: Also on March 26, 1991, Singapore Airlines Flight 117 was hijacked by four armed men and forced to land at Changi Airport. In a rare outcome for such crises, all passengers were freed in a swift night assault by Singapore's commandos, ending the standoff in barely half a minute.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A hijacked flight and an episode about our dependence on invisible networks both probe how control can be seized or lost in modern systems that usually feel routine and safe.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1997, authorities discovered 39 bodies in a mansion near San Diego: members of the Heaven's Gate cult who had committed ritual mass suicide, believing a spacecraft behind comet Hale–Bopp would transform them into a higher existence. Their farewell videos and matching outfits turned a sci‑fi fantasy into one of the most unsettling pop‑culture tragedies of the late 20th century.

episode.title: Morning Drops #10 - The One With Many Names
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the glamorous world of Ghost, the Swedish band that turned rock into ritual. From masked clergy to shifting identities, diving into the music and myth of one of the most theatrical acts of our time.

published.match_reason: A UFO‑centric cult blending ritual, costume, and apocalyptic belief parallels an episode about Ghost, a band that plays with religious imagery and theatrical myth, though here safely in the realm of art.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 2005, between 200,000 and 300,000 people filled the streets of Taipei to protest China’s Anti‑Secession Law, which authorized "non‑peaceful" means to stop Taiwanese independence. The march was a sea of color and homemade signs, a reminder that abstract legal texts can send real people outdoors to stand together.

episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
